Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 14
Recent Green phase: added high-ench surcharge (0.3 * base) per item with enchantment >= 5; introduced itemPremium accumulator separate from policy basePrice

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` in `src/claim-office.ts`; `quote()` now returns `PROCESSING_FEE`
5	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `5` is the domain "processing fee" from the spec; naming it removes a magic number
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant); `quote(input)` public function kept as-is — name still fits
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- No refactoring (justified): only 2 data points (empty=5, single potion newcomer=49); the literal `49` is hardcoded and any decomposition into `base + first-insurance + fee` components would be speculative triangulation beyond what tests demand. Naming `49` (e.g. `POTION_NEWCOMER_PREMIUM`) would not add intent beyond the test name and would clutter the module before the algorithm emerges in cycle 3+ (amulet test). `quote` name still fits.
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
13	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
14	- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- Change: decomposed opaque per-type totals into the domain formula. `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (49/71/93) → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (40/60/80) plus new constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. `quote` now computes `basePrice + basePrice * 0.1 + PROCESSING_FEE` for non-empty item lists.
18	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — with the 3rd data point (staff 80→93) in place, all three test names explicitly state the same `base + first-insurance + fee` decomposition; the implementation now mirrors the spec language instead of hiding the formula behind precomputed totals. Also Rule 3 (DRY): the surcharge+fee pattern was conceptually duplicated across all three map entries; now expressed once. Mass increased ~25→~33 — accepted because Rule 2 > APP. Verified all 3 base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword case (100) yield integer results under `base*0.1`, so no rounding decision was committed (rounding rule for rune 25→33 deferred to its own cycle).
19	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (replaces `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`), `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (new domain constant); `quote` name still fits
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): sword test added one row (`sword: 100`) to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` — the cycle-4 decomposition absorbed it without any structural change. Considered inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * (1 + RATE)` for Rule 4 (fewest elements), rejected because test names enumerate three distinct components ("100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee") and Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP. Considered extracting a `priceForFirstItem(type)` helper, rejected as speculative — no second-item or modifier test active yet. `quote` name still fits.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` so the final return reads `return Math.ceil(premium)` instead of `Math.ceil(basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE)`. Also added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (in Green); the structural fit was already there.
26	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the rune test introduced rounding-up as a domain rule (25 × 1.1 + 5 = 32.5 → 33). Naming `premium` makes the rounding step a visible, named operation ("round the premium up to a whole gold piece") rather than a wrapper around a raw arithmetic expression. Mass +1 binding (~35→36), accepted on the cycle-4 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when test vocabulary ("premium ... rounded up") explicitly names the value. Considered leaving the inline form for Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected because the rounding rule's target deserves a name now that rounding exists. Considered renaming `quote` itself (e.g. to `computePremium`) — rejected, "quote" is the domain action and the function still returns the quoted premium amount.
27	- Naming established: `premium` (local binding for the pre-rounded total); `quote` and existing constants unchanged.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: removed the empty-items early-return guard (`if (input.items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;`). With cycle-7 Green generalising `basePrice` to `items.reduce(..., 0)`, the empty case now falls out of the same formula: `0 + 0*0.1 + 5 = 5` (and `Math.ceil(5) = 5`). One uniform code path handles all item counts.
31	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the special case became dead code the moment `basePrice` became an accumulator over `items`. Also nets Rule 2 (reveals intent): a single formula expresses the domain rule "premium = ceil(sum-of-base-prices × 1.1 + processing fee)" for every item-count, rather than splitting "empty" and "non-empty" branches. Mass drop ~−9 (one conditional, one length access, one equality, one literal `0`). Considered keeping the guard for "documentation value" — rejected: the empty test name ("empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)") is now an emergent consequence of the general formula, which is stronger evidence of correctness than a hand-coded shortcut. Naming all still fits.
32	- Naming established: none new; removed elements only.
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- Change: extracted the literal `60` in the 3-runes guard into module-level constant `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`. Body now reads `isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice` instead of `... ? 60 : rawBasePrice`. Implementation shape (narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes`) left intact.
36	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `60` is the domain "building block price for 3 runes" per Example Mapping; naming removes a magic number and the visual collision with `amulet: 60` (different concepts, same value). Mass +1 (one new constant binding at module scope), accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `hasRuneBuildingBlock` and generalising to a per-block discount (would scale to cycles 9-12: 4 runes, 7 runes, mixed rune/moonstone). Rejected as speculative — next test ("4 runes → no block") will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation. The narrow guard's name remains accurate for the single data point we have. Considered also extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named predicate function — rejected, it's used once and inlining preserves locality.
37	- Naming established: `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant); `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `quote`, and other existing names unchanged.
38	
39	## After cycle 9
40	- Change: extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to remove three repeated `input.items` accesses across the reduce, the length check, and the every-call. Body now reads `items.reduce(...)`, `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`.
41	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) — three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~−3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change — the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it — confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. Considered (and rejected) generalising the rune-block logic to `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks now; that is speculative triangulation. The 7-runes cycle (cycle 10, currently `it.todo` "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base") is the genuine forcing test for that generalisation. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes`: name still accurate after a negative-case confirmation. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function or inlining `rawBasePrice`: both rejected on cycle-8 precedent (locality / verbose-expression-deserves-a-name respectively).
42	- Naming established: `items` (local binding aliasing `input.items`); no other name changes.
43	
44	## After cycle 10
45	- No refactoring (justified): the 7-runes test (expected base 175 = 7 × 25, premium 198) passed without any implementation change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `items.length === 3` excludes the 7-rune case from the block-discount path, so `rawBasePrice` (175) flows straight through `basePrice` and the existing formula yields ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = ceil(197.5) = 198. Important observation: the test's prose ("1 block of 3 + 4 individuals → 175 G base") describes the *narrative* but the *numeric expectation* (175 = 7 × 25, no discount) confirms that the block discount only applies when exactly 3 runes are insured — making this the SECOND confirming test for the narrow guard, not a forcing test. The genuine forcing test for `floor(runeCount/3)` generalisation is cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base"), where two distinct per-type blocks must each be priced at 60. Generalising now (computing `floor(count/3)` blocks across all runes only) would still need restructuring once moonstones arrive, so it's premature. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock` (consequence-oriented over structure-oriented, marginal Rule 2 gain) — rejected because cycle 12 will force the predicate to change shape entirely (per-type rune+moonstone block counting), so any rename now is churn. Considered inlining `rawBasePrice` into the ternary or collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * 1.1` — both rejected on cycle-4/cycle-5 precedent that test-name vocabulary ("base + first-insurance + fee") earns each value its own named binding (Rule 2 > APP). Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function — rejected on cycle-8 precedent (used once, inlining preserves locality). All existing names still fit.
46	
47	## After cycle 11
48	- Change: reordered module-level constants by domain role. Now grouped as `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (with the new `moonstone: 25` entry added in Green) + `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` first (item-pricing data), followed by `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` + `PROCESSING_FEE` (premium-formula constants). Body of `quote` unchanged.
49	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the new ordering mirrors the function's computational flow (lookup base prices → maybe apply block override → apply surcharge → add fee), making the data shape easier to scan as cycle 12 will introduce per-type block-pricing logic. Mass change: 0 (pure reorder). The "2 runes + 1 moonstone → no block" test is the THIRD confirming test for the narrow `isThreeAlikeRunes` guard (after cycles 9 and 10): the `every((item) => item.type === "rune")` predicate correctly excludes the mixed-type case, so no structural change was needed. Cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base") remains the genuine forcing test that will restructure the predicate into per-type block counting. Considered (and rejected) generalising the predicate now — would be triangulation on three confirming negatives. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a function or renaming it — rejected on cycle-8/10 precedent (used once, cycle-12 will reshape it). Considered deduplicating `rune: 25, moonstone: 25` via a shared constant — rejected as false coupling (the two types share a price by spec coincidence, not by definition). All existing names still fit.
50	- Naming established: none new; constant order is the only change.
51	
52	## After cycle 12
53	- Change: clarified the rune-component block-discount vocabulary. (1) Renamed `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` → `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE` — the cycle-12 Green generalised the rule to apply to both runes AND moonstones, so the "THREE_RUNES" name had become a lie. (2) Extracted `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"])` to name the spec category "rune-class building components". (3) Extracted `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE = 3` to name the domain literal (exactly 3 of a kind forms a block). (4) Extracted local `const formsRuneBlock = RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` to name the per-type-group predicate that the cycle-12 Green collapsed into the inline `if`.
54	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — after cycle 12, the inline guard `(type === "rune" || type === "moonstone") && count === 3` mixed three domain concepts (the rune-component type category, the block size, the block-formation predicate) as anonymous literals. Each now has a name that mirrors the spec language ("rune components", "block of three"). The renamed constant also resolves the stale "THREE_RUNES" name that no longer matched the moonstone case. Considered also generalising to `floor(count/blockSize)` blocks per type (would support a future "6 runes = 2 blocks at 120 G" test) — rejected as speculative triangulation; the current tests only pin down "exactly 3 of a kind". Considered renaming `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES` to something more general like `BLOCK_ELIGIBLE_TYPES` — rejected, "rune component" is the spec's noun phrase for runes+moonstones (per Example Mapping). Considered extracting a `priceForTypeGroup(type, count)` helper function — rejected on cycle-8/10 precedent (used once, locality preserved by inline ternary-ish branch). Mass change: ~+5 (3 new constant bindings + 1 local binding + 1 invocation `.has()` swapped in for `||`+`===`+`===` — net mass roughly neutral; accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent). All structural shape (countByType + per-type-group loop + branch) preserved.
55	- Naming established: `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES` (Set replacing inline type union), `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` (replaces literal `3`), `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE` (renames `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE`), `formsRuneBlock` (local per-type-group predicate); `quote`, `countByType`, `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`, `items`, `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `PROCESSING_FEE` unchanged.
56	
57	## After cycle 13
58	- No refactoring (justified): the "sword enchantment 4, not cursed → 115 G" test passed immediately with zero implementation change — `enchantment` and `cursed` fields are not yet read, and the boundary-below-threshold case `4 < 5` correctly exercises the existing path (`sword: 100` base → 115 G premium) unchanged. This is the FIRST confirming test for the upcoming high-enchantment-surcharge rule, NOT its forcing test. Cycle 14 ("enchantment exactly 5 → +30 G") is the genuine forcing test for the high-enchantment surcharge; cycle 15 ("cursed → +50 G") is the forcing test for the curse surcharge. Considered widening the `items` type from `{ type: string }[]` to `{ type: string; enchantment?: number; cursed?: boolean }[]` to honour what the test actually passes — rejected as premature; the type should grow when those fields are actually consumed (cycle 14), and adding optional fields now with no reader would be speculative. Considered pre-extracting a `surchargesFor(item)` helper returning 0 — rejected as pure YAGNI. Considered extracting a per-type-group `priceForGroup(type, count)` helper, collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge`/`premium`/`items` locals, or renaming `quote` — all rejected on prior-cycle precedent (cycles 4, 6, 8, 10: test vocabulary earns each value its own name; locality preserved when used once; "quote" is the domain action). All existing names still fit. Mass unchanged.
59

content.text:
All 14 tests passing. Now I'll evaluate refactoring opportunities systematically.

## Refactoring Evaluation

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST priority)

The Green phase added a new accumulator `itemPremium` that holds `basePrice + sum-of-per-item-surcharges`. Let me evaluate names:

- `itemPremium` — Misleading. It's NOT the full item premium (it excludes first-insurance and processing fee). It's "base price + per-item surcharges". A better name would reflect that it's the item-level subtotal before policy-level adjustments.
- `basePrice` — Still accurate (per-type aggregate using block discount).
- `firstInsuranceSurcharge` — Still uses `basePrice` (correct: surcharge is on policy base, not item-surcharged subtotal).
- `premium` — Still names the pre-rounded total.
- `quote` — Still the domain action.

The new `itemPremium` is the suspicious name. Examining what it represents: the sum of `(basePrice)` and per-item enchantment surcharges. In the spec language (Example Mapping), these are "item-level surcharges" added to the per-type base. A clearer name: `itemTotal` or `itemTotalWithSurcharges` or split into `basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges`.

### Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office — Example Mapping
2	
3	## Rules
4	
5	### R1 — Item base premiums (main items)
6	Each main item has a fixed insurance value and base premium:
7	- Sword: 1000 G value, 100 G base premium
8	- Amulet: 600 G value, 60 G base premium
9	- Staff: 800 G value, 80 G base premium
10	- Potion: 400 G value, 40 G base premium
11	
12	### R2 — Component base premiums
13	Components (e.g. runes, moonstones): 250 G insurance value, 25 G base premium each.
14	
15	### R3 — Building block of 3 alike components
16	A block of **exactly 3 alike** components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G (instead of 75 G). Block requires exactly 3; 4 alike means 100 G (no block applies). 6 alike = 2 blocks = 120 G. 7 alike = 1 block + 4 individuals = 60 + 100 = 175 G. "Alike" means **same type** (runes with runes, moonstones with moonstones — not mixed).
17	
18	### R4 — Cursed surcharge (item-specific)
19	A cursed item adds 50% surcharge to that **item's** base premium.
20	
21	### R5 — High enchantment surcharge (item-specific)
22	Items with enchantment level ≥ 5 add a 30% surcharge to that item's base premium.
23	
24	### R6 — Loyalty discount (policy-wide)
25	Customers with ≥ 2 years with MHPCO get a 20% loyalty discount on the policy base premium.
26	
27	### R7 — First insurance surcharge (item-specific)
28	Each item in a quote carries a 10% initial assessment surcharge applied to its base premium. Each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance regardless of customer history.
29	
30	### R8 — Follow-up contract discount (policy-wide)
31	Customers receive a 15% discount on each contract **after their first** (scenario-scoped: first quote in scenario gets no follow-up discount; subsequent quotes do).
32	
33	### R9 — Processing fee
34	A 5 G processing fee is added to **every** premium at the very end.
35	
36	### R10 — Modifier scope and stacking order
37	- Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment, first insurance) apply to that item's base premium.
38	- Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, follow-up contract) apply to the sum of item base premiums (policy base premium).
39	- Processing fee added last.
40	- Surcharges and discounts on the same base are additive (a percent of the base), not multiplicative.
41	
42	### R11 — Premium rounding
43	The final premium is rounded **up** to whole G (MHPCO's favor). Intermediate amounts are kept as fractions.
44	
45	### R12 — Deductible
46	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event (per entry in `damages`).
47	
48	### R13 — High enchantment payout clause
49	Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50% of the damage amount (before deductible).
50	
51	### R14 — Dragon material payout clause
52	Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed (then deductible).
53	
54	### R15 — Clause precedence
55	When both R13 and R14 apply, the 50% rule wins (then deductible).
56	
57	### R16 — Payout rounding
58	The final payout is rounded **down** to whole G (MHPCO's favor).
59	
60	### R17 — Multiple items of same type
61	A policy may cover multiple items of the same type. Each entry in `damages` is a separate event with its own deductible. If `damages` references more items of a type than are insured, the whole claim is rejected (CLI exits non-zero).
62	
63	### R18 — Empty quote
64	An empty item list yields premium = 5 G (processing fee only).
65	
66	### R19 — Unknown item type in quote
67	Quote with unknown item type → CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr, no `results` written.
68	
69	### R20 — Claim references item not in policy
70	Claim references item not in policy (wrong type, or unknown type) → CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr.
71	
72	### R21 — Negative damage amount
73	Damage entry with `amount: -200` → CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr.
74	
75	### R22 — CLI scenario processing
76	The CLI reads a JSON scenario from stdin, processes `steps` sequentially. Claims reference earlier quote steps by zero-based index in `policy`. Writes `{results: [...]}` to stdout, with one entry per step (`{premium}` or `{payout}`).
77	
78	## Examples
79	
80	### Example E1 — Components, building blocks

content.text:
R10 confirms: item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment, first insurance) apply to that item's base premium. Item-specific modifiers are conceptually similar — they all surcharge the per-item base.

Looking at the spec language and the code, the `itemPremium` accumulator combines:
- `basePrice` (sum of per-type base prices, with block discount)
- per-item high-enchantment surcharges

This is conceptually "item base + item-specific surcharges accumulated per item". Per R10, "first insurance" is also an item-specific surcharge applied per item — but in the code, `firstInsuranceSurcharge` is computed at the policy level as `basePrice * 0.1`. That works only because 10% is a constant rate that distributes over sums; mathematically `sum(item.base * 0.1) = sum(item.base) * 0.1 = basePrice * 0.1`. So the current `firstInsuranceSurcharge` formula is fine.

The bigger naming/structure question: `itemPremium` currently means "base price + high-enchantment surcharges". After `cursed` surcharge arrives in cycle 16, it will likely accumulate cursed surcharges too. So this accumulator's role is "base price + item-specific non-rate-distributable surcharges". A more apt name today: `itemSubtotal` or split it into clearer pieces.

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1: Tests pass** — All 14 passing.

**Rule 2: Reveals intent** — `itemPremium` name is misleading (it's not the premium). Also: the current code computes `basePrice`, then separately accumulates `itemPremium`. The relationship `itemPremium = basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges` is implicit. Making it explicit would help future readers and align with R10 (item-specific surcharges).

**Rule 3: No duplication** — The `for (const item of items)` loop now runs twice (once in `countByType`, once for enchantment surcharges). They couldn't easily merge because countByType is needed before the per-type block logic. Not worth merging.

**Rule 4: Fewest elements** — The `itemPremium` variable is essentially `basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges`. Splitting it into a named `highEnchantmentSurcharges` accumulator would add a name but also align with R5 vocabulary.

### Decision

Rename `itemPremium` to be split as a separate `highEnchantmentSurcharges` accumulator, then compose explicitly. This:
- Names the per-item surcharge total using R5's exact spec vocabulary ("high enchantment surcharge")
- Makes the formula `basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` mirror R10's stacking order verbatim

### Step 5: APP Mass comparison (focus on the changed region)

**Before** (just the changed lines, the high-enchantment block + premium assembly):
- `let itemPremium = basePrice` — 1 binding + 1 assign-ish (binding init counted as binding) = ~1
- `for (const item of items)` — 1 loop, 1 binding = 6
- `if ((item.enchantment ?? 0) >= 5)` — 1 conditional + 1 invocation (`??`) + 1 constant `0` + 1 constant `5` + bindings/invocations = ~7
- `itemPremium += BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[item.type] * 0.3` — 1 assignment + 1 invocation (`[]`) + 1 constant `0.3` + 1 invocation (`*`) = ~10
- `const firstInsuranceSurcharge = basePrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` — 1 binding + 1 invocation = 3
- `const premium = itemPremium + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` — 1 binding + 2 invocations = 5
- `return Math.ceil(premium)` — 1 invocation = 2

Subtotal: ~34

**After**:
- `let highEnchantmentSurcharges = 0` — 1 binding + 1 constant = 2
- Loop body — same shape: 1 loop + 1 binding + 1 conditional + invocations + constants ≈ same as before (literals `5` and `0.3` are now named constants, so they count as bindings instead of constants — same mass weight: 1 each)
- `highEnchantmentSurcharges += BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[item.type] * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` — 1 assignment + 2 invocations = ~8 (similar)
- `firstInsuranceSurcharge` line — unchanged = 3
- `const premium = basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` — 1 binding + 3 invocations = 7 (one extra `+` compared to before)
- `return Math.ceil(premium)` — 2

Module-level additions: 2 new constants (`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`) = 2 bindings + 2 constant init = ~4 mass.

Net mass change: **roughly +5** (two new named constants, one extra `+` in the premium formula). Accepted on the cycle-2/4/6/8/12 precedent: **Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP** when test/spec vocabulary explicitly names the values.

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
1. Renamed `itemPremium` (misleading — it wasn't the premium) into separately-accumulated `highEnchantmentSurcharges` (matches R5's spec noun phrase exactly).
2. Made the final premium assembly explicit: `basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` — this mirrors R10's stacking order line-for-line.
3. Extracted magic literals `5` and `0.3` into named constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (per R5).

**Benefits:**
- Eliminates the misleading `itemPremium` name (it was not a premium).
- The premium formula now reads as a transparent sum of named components matching R10.
- Sets up clean shape for cycle 16 (cursed surcharge) — it will add a parallel `cursedSurcharges` accumulator in the same loop, slotted into the same `premium` sum.
- Named threshold/rate match R5's spec language ("enchantment level ≥ 5", "30% surcharge").

### Step 7: Tests Status

All 14 tests passing. Refactor complete.

### Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

## After cycle 13
- No refactoring (justified): the "sword enchantment 4, not cursed → 115 G" test passed immediately with zero implementation change — `enchantment` and `cursed` fields are not yet read, and the boundary-below-threshold case `4 < 5` correctly exercises the existing path (`sword: 100` base → 115 G premium) unchanged. This is the FIRST confirming test for the upcoming high-enchantment-surcharge rule, NOT its forcing test. Cycle 14 ("enchantment exactly 5 → +30 G") is the genuine forcing test for the high-enchantment surcharge; cycle 15 ("cursed → +50 G") is the forcing test for the curse surcharge. Considered widening the `items` type from `{ type: string }[]` to `{ type: string; enchantment?: number; cursed?: boolean }[]` to honour what the test actually passes — rejected as premature; the type should grow when those fields are actually consumed (cycle 14), and adding optional fields now with no reader would be speculative. input.new_string:
## After cycle 13
- No refactoring (justified): the "sword enchantment 4, not cursed → 115 G" test passed immediately with zero implementation change — `enchantment` and `cursed` fields are not yet read, and the boundary-below-threshold case `4 < 5` correctly exercises the existing path (`sword: 100` base → 115 G premium) unchanged. This is the FIRST confirming test for the upcoming high-enchantment-surcharge rule, NOT its forcing test. Cycle 14 ("enchantment exactly 5 → +30 G") is the genuine forcing test for the high-enchantment surcharge; cycle 15 ("cursed → +50 G") is the forcing test for the curse surcharge. Considered widening the `items` type from `{ type: string }[]` to `{ type: string; enchantment?: number; cursed?: boolean }[]` to honour what the test actually passes — rejected as premature; the type should grow when those fields are actually consumed (cycle 14), and adding optional fields now with no reader would be speculative. Considered pre-extracting a `surchargesFor(item)` helper returning 0 — rejected as pure YAGNI. Considered extracting a per-type-group `priceForGroup(type, count)` helper, collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge`/`premium`/`items` locals, or renaming `quote` — all rejected on prior-cycle precedent (cycles 4, 6, 8, 10: test vocabulary earns each value its own name; locality preserved when used once; "quote" is the domain action). All existing names still fit. Mass unchanged.

## After cycle 14
- Change: three coordinated improvements after the high-enchantment-surcharge forcing test (sword ench 5 → 145 G) went green. (1) Renamed misleading local `itemPremium` (which held `basePrice + per-item enchantment surcharges` — never a "premium") into a separate `highEnchantmentSurcharges` accumulator initialised to 0; the per-item loop now adds only the surcharge contribution. (2) Made the premium assembly explicit and ordered to mirror R10: `const premium = basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE`. (3) Extracted module-level `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` (grouped above the existing policy-formula constants), removing two magic numbers (`5`, `0.3`).
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the post-Green `itemPremium` name was a lie (it was not a premium, just a mid-pipeline subtotal that conflated base price with one specific item-level surcharge). The new shape names each surcharge family separately, and the explicit four-summand `premium` line reads as R10's stacking order verbatim ("item base + item-specific surcharges + first-insurance surcharge + processing fee"). The constants match R5's exact spec phrasing ("enchantment level ≥ 5", "30% surcharge"). Setup benefit for cycle 16 (cursed surcharge): it will drop in as a parallel `cursedSurcharges` accumulator in the same per-item loop, slotted into the same `premium` sum, with no restructuring. Considered merging the two `for (const item of items)` loops (countByType + high-ench) into one pass for Rule 3 (DRY) — rejected: the block-discount step needs `countByType` fully built before computing `basePrice`, and only then can per-item-base lookups be meaningful; the two loops solve different sub-problems despite looping over the same collection. Considered keeping `itemPremium` as a rolling subtotal and only adding a comment — rejected: misleading names are a smell that comments cannot fix. Considered NOT naming `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`/`_RATE` yet ("one data point") — rejected: cycle 13 (boundary 4) plus cycle 14 (exactly 5) together pin down both the threshold and the rate as named domain concepts, and the next two todos (cycles 15-16) reuse these exact values in test names ("enchantment 5", "30 high-ench"). Mass change: ~+5 (two new constants, one extra `+` in the premium line, one new binding `highEnchantmentSurcharges`; offset by literals→named-constants which keep mass weight the same). Accepted on cycle-4/6/8/12 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the values.
- Naming established: `highEnchantmentSurcharges` (local per-item-surcharge accumulator, replaces misleading `itemPremium`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (module-private constant, replaces literal `5`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.3`); `quote`, `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`, `items`, `countByType`, and all R3/R7/R9 constants unchanged.

2. Made the premium assembly explicit — `basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` now mirrors R10's stacking order verbatim.
3. Extracted `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` as named module-level constants (replacing two magic numbers, matching R5's spec phrasing).

**Mass Change**: roughly +5 (two new named constants, one extra `+` in the premium sum, one new local binding). Accepted on the precedent of cycles 4, 6, 8, 12: Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the values.
